---
title: "World Development Indicators (2022): A Quick Exploratory Analysis"
author: "Oliver Xie"
date: "2026-02-25"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Overview

This report analyzes a 2022 cross-section of the World Development Indicators (WDI) dataset.  
We focus on three indicators:

- GDP per capita (current US$)
- Life expectancy (years)
- Unemployment rate (% of total labor force)

The dataset comes from the World Bank’s WDI program [@worldbank_wdi]. We also reference the classic “Preston curve” relationship between income and health outcomes [@preston1975].

## Data loading

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_csv("wdi.csv")
df.columns = [c.strip().lower() for c in df.columns]

# Keep only the columns we use in this report
cols = ["country", "gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy", "unemployment_rate", "total_population"]
d = df[cols].copy()

# Basic cleaning: remove rows missing key values for core analysis
d_core = d.dropna(subset=["gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy", "unemployment_rate"]).copy()

d.shape, d_core.shape
```


## Exploratory Data Analysis
### GDP per capita
```{python}
d["gdp_per_capita"].describe()
```

Countries vary dramatically in GDP per capita, which motivates using logs.

### Life expectancy
```{python}
d["life_expectancy"].describe()
```

Life expectancy shows less dispersion than GDP but still meaningful variation.

### Unemployment rate
```{python}
d["unemployment_rate"].describe()
```

Unemployment rates show substantial dispersion.

## Visualizations
### GDP per capita vs Life expectancy
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = d_core["gdp_per_capita"]
y = d_core["life_expectancy"]
```

```{python echo=false}
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(x, y)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_title("GDP per Capita vs Life Expectancy (2022)")
ax.set_xlabel("GDP per capita (current US$, log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy (years)")
plt.show()
```
::: {#fig-gdp-life}
Figure: GDP per capita and life expectancy (log scale).  
Source: World Bank WDI (2022).
:::

### Top 10 Countries by GDP per Capita
```{python}
top10 = d.dropna(subset=["gdp_per_capita"]).sort_values("gdp_per_capita", ascending=False).head(10)
top10[["country", "gdp_per_capita"]]
```
```{python echo=false}
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(top10["country"], top10["gdp_per_capita"])
ax.set_title("Top 10 Countries by GDP per Capita (2022)")
ax.set_xlabel("Country")
ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita (current US$)")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=60)
plt.show()
```
::: {#fig-top10-gdp}
Figure: Top 10 countries by GDP per capita.  
Source: World Bank WDI (2022).
:::

## Summary Table
```{python}
summary = d_core[["gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy", "unemployment_rate"]].describe().T
summary = summary[["count", "mean", "std", "min", "50%", "max"]]
summary
```
::: {#tbl-summary}
Table: Descriptive statistics for selected indicators.
:::
## Conclusion

GDP per capita is strongly associated with life expectancy (see @fig-gdp-life).  
The income distribution is highly skewed (see @fig-top10-gdp).  
Key statistics are summarized in @tbl-summary.

## References
